content.text: What Is an AWS Account?

An AWS account is the fundamental unit of isolation in Amazon Web Services. It is not merely a login — it is a complete, independent container that holds all of your resources, identities, billing information, and configuration. Every service you launch, every IAM user you create, every VPC you configure exists within exactly one account.

The account boundary is a hard security boundary enforced by AWS itself, not just by policies. A resource in Account A cannot access a resource in Account B unless an explicit cross-account trust relationship is established through IAM roles. This makes the account boundary the strongest isolation mechanism AWS provides — stronger than VPCs, stronger than IAM policies, stronger than resource-level permissions.

Every AWS account receives a globally unique 12-digit Account ID (for example, 123456789012) at creation time. This ID never changes and is used throughout AWS to identify the account in ARNs (Amazon Resource Names), cross-account role trust policies, CloudTrail logs, Cost Explorer, and AWS Support cases.
Single Account vs Multi-Account Architecture

attrs.alt: Single account structure vs AWS Organizations multi-account architecture

content.text: The diagram shows both patterns side by side. On the left, a single account holds all resources and environments with IAM as the only boundary between them. On the right, AWS Organizations orchestrates a hierarchy of accounts grouped into Organizational Units (OUs) with Service Control Policies applied at each level.

The choice between single-account and multi-account architecture is one of the most consequential structural decisions in AWS. It is not a question of complexity preference — it is a question of what risk and compliance constraints apply to your workloads.
Single-Account Architecture

A single account is the natural starting point for individual developers, small teams, and projects where strict environment isolation is not required. All resources live together — development databases alongside production databases, test IAM users alongside production IAM users — separated only by naming conventions and IAM policies.

The advantages: simplicity, a single bill, no cross-account IAM complexity, and no need to manage multiple sets of credentials or assume roles to access resources. For a solo developer building a personal project or a startup with three engineers, a single account is entirely appropriate.

The risks: if an IAM credential is compromised, the blast radius includes all environments and all resources. A misconfigured IAM policy can accidentally grant access to production data while targeting development data. Service limits (such as maximum EC2 instances or Lambda concurrency) are shared across all environments — a spike in the development environment can exhaust quota that production depends on.
Multi-Account Architecture with AWS Organizations

In a multi-account architecture, each environment (dev, staging, production) runs in a separate AWS account. These accounts are linked through AWS Organizations, which provides centralized governance, consolidated billing, and the ability to apply Service Control Policies across the entire account hierarchy.

The blast radius advantage is the most compelling reason to adopt multi-account: a compromised development account credential cannot access production resources because they are in a different account with an independent IAM namespace. Deleting a production database in a development account is impossible by definition. Service limits are scoped per account, so development throughput spikes cannot starve production.

The tradeoff: more operational complexity. You need to manage multiple sets of credentials (mitigated by IAM Identity Center, covered later), understand cross-account role assumption patterns, and set up shared services (logging, security tooling, networking) that span accounts. For teams beyond a few engineers, or any organization with compliance requirements (SOC 2, PCI, HIPAA), this complexity is justified.
AWS Organizations: The Account Hierarchy

AWS Organizations is the service that manages multiple AWS accounts as a single entity. It provides three core capabilities: account creation and management, Organizational Unit (OU) hierarchy for grouping accounts, and Service Control Policies (SCPs) for applying permission guardrails.
Organizational Units

OUs are folders that group accounts within an organization. Accounts in an OU inherit all SCPs applied to that OU, plus SCPs applied to any parent OUs, up to the organization root. This creates a layered permission model where broad guardrails apply at the top and more specific ones apply at lower levels.
A common OU structure for a mid-size company:
Root:
The top of the hierarchy. SCPs here apply to all accounts in the organization.
OU: Security:
Contains the log archive account (all CloudTrail, Config, and GuardDuty logs aggregated here) and the security tooling account (centralized Security Hub, Inspector).
OU: Infrastructure:
Shared networking account (Transit Gateway, Direct Connect), shared services (Route 53 private hosted zones, Active Directory).
OU: Workloads:
Subdivided by application or team, each with Dev/Staging/Prod accounts.
OU: Sandbox:
Developer experimentation accounts with aggressive SCPs that prevent production-like configurations.
The OU structure is not just organizational — it directly determines which SCPs apply to which accounts. Placing an account in the wrong OU means it inherits the wrong set of guardrails.
Service Control Policies

SCPs are permission guardrails that constrain what IAM identities in member accounts can do, regardless of what IAM policies grant them. An SCP is evaluated before IAM policies — if the SCP denies an action, no IAM policy can allow it.

SCPs do not grant permissions; they only define the maximum permissions available. An SCP that allows all actions (the default), combined with an IAM policy that denies S3 deletions, means S3 deletions are denied. But an SCP that denies S3 in a particular region cannot be overridden by an IAM policy that grants S3 access — the SCP wins.

This SCP prevents any IAM identity in the accounts it is applied to from provisioning resources outside the three approved regions. Even if a developer attempts to launch an EC2 instance in ap-southeast-1, the SCP rejects the request before IAM evaluation. This is a common compliance control for organizations that have data residency requirements.
Core Properties of an AWS Account
Account ID
The 12-digit Account ID is the immutable identifier for an account. It appears in ARNs (
arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket
is a global resource, but
arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/alice

is account-scoped), in cross-account role trust policies, and in the AWS Console URL for IAM role switching. You cannot change an account ID, and it persists even if you change the account's email address, root user password, or account name.
Regions Are Scoped Per Account

AWS Regions are independent geographic areas (us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-northeast-1, etc.). Each region has its own completely independent set of resources, configuration, IAM service (though IAM is global, the resources it protects are regional), and CloudTrail trails. When you launch an EC2 instance in us-east-1, it does not appear in the us-west-2 console — each region has its own resource inventory.

This regional independence is valuable for fault isolation but creates an operational challenge: resources across regions must be managed explicitly. CloudTrail, Config, and GuardDuty each need to be enabled per region to provide coverage. A common mistake is enabling security tooling only in the primary region and assuming it covers the whole account.
Service Quotas Are Per Account Per Region

Every AWS service has default limits on how many resources you can provision, called service quotas or service limits. These quotas are independent per account and per region. The default EC2 running instance limit in us-east-1 for one account does not affect the limit in another account, and does not affect the limit in eu-west-1 of the same account.

Service quota independence is one of the practical reasons to prefer multi-account architectures. In a single account, a development environment that hits the Lambda concurrency limit (1,000 concurrent executions by default) can starve production Lambda functions of capacity. In separate accounts, each has its own independent quota that the other cannot consume.
Billing Boundary

Each AWS account generates its own independent usage and billing data. In a standalone account, this is your monthly AWS invoice. In an AWS Organization, each account's usage is visible individually in Cost Explorer, but the management account receives a consolidated invoice covering all member accounts with any volume discounts applied across the aggregate spend.

Consolidated billing means that Reserved Instances and Savings Plans purchased in one account can be shared across the organization — a significant cost optimization for organizations with predictable workloads. An RI purchased in the production account can cover matching instance usage in the development account if the shared benefit is not disabled.
Creating and Configuring an Account
Direct Account Creation

Creating a standalone AWS account requires an email address, a phone number for verification, a credit card (even for Free Tier accounts), and basic contact information. The entire process takes under 10 minutes, and basic services are available immediately. More advanced services (EC2 capacity in certain regions, some specialized services) may require a brief activation period of up to 24 hours.
After creation, the essential first steps are:
Enable MFA on the root user immediately
Enable IAM billing access so IAM users can view costs
Create an IAM identity for day-to-day use rather than using root
Enable CloudTrail to begin logging all API activity
Set up a billing budget alert at $0.01 to detect any unexpected charges
Account Creation Through AWS Organizations

Member accounts created through AWS Organizations are provisioned programmatically via the Organizations API, the AWS console, or tools like AWS Control Tower. The account is automatically linked to the organization and inherits all applicable SCPs.

# Create a new member account in AWS Organizations
aws organizations create-account \
  --email prod-account@company.com \
  --account-name "MyApp-Production" \
  --iam-user-access-to-billing ALLOW

# Check creation status (account creation is async)
aws organizations describe-create-account-status \
  --create-account-request-id <request-id>

# Move the new account into the correct OU
aws organizations move-account \
  --account-id <new-account-id> \
  --source-parent-id <root-id> \
  --destination-parent-id <workloads-ou-id>
The
--iam-user-access-to-billing ALLOW
flag is important: it ensures that IAM users with appropriate permissions can view billing information in the new account. Without it, billing data is visible only to the root user.
Account creation through Organizations automatically creates a cross-account role named
OrganizationAccountAccessRole

in the new member account, with the management account as the trusted principal. This role allows the management account's IAM identities to assume into the new account for initial configuration — before any local IAM users or roles have been created in it.
AWS Control Tower

AWS Control Tower automates the setup of a multi-account landing zone. It provisions the foundational accounts (management, log archive, audit), creates a recommended OU structure, enables organization-level CloudTrail and Config, and applies a set of pre-built governance controls called guardrails. Control Tower reduces what would be days of manual setup into a guided process that takes a few hours.

For organizations building their first multi-account environment, Control Tower is the recommended starting point. It enforces best practices by default and provides a solid foundation that can be extended with additional SCPs and customizations.
Account Limits and Quotas Management

Service quotas are the per-account-per-region limits on resource counts, API call rates, and other service parameters. Understanding and managing quotas is important for production workloads that may approach default limits.

# List service quotas for a specific service
aws service-quotas list-service-quotas --service-code ec2

# Get the current applied quota for a specific limit
aws service-quotas get-service-quota \
  --service-code ec2 \
  --quota-code L-1216C47A  # Running On-Demand Standard instances

# Request a quota increase
aws service-quotas request-service-quota-increase \
  --service-code lambda \
  --quota-code L-B99A9384 \
  --desired-value 3000

# List pending quota increase requests
aws service-quotas list-requested-changes-by-service \
  --service-code lambda

Quota increase requests for most services are approved automatically within minutes if the requested value is within normal bounds. Requests for very large increases (multi-fold increases in EC2 or Lambda concurrency) may require manual AWS review, which can take 1–2 business days. Submit quota increase requests before you need them, not when you are blocked by a limit.
Closing an AWS Account

Closing an AWS account is a root-user-only operation with significant consequences. The process is irreversible after a brief reconsideration window, and all resources in the account are permanently deleted.
When an account is closed:
All running instances, databases, and other resources are terminated
All data (S3 objects, EBS volumes, DynamoDB tables) is permanently deleted after 90 days
The account enters a 'suspended' state for 90 days where it cannot be used but can be reopened if needed
After 90 days, the account is permanently closed and the resources are permanently deleted
The account ID may eventually be reassigned to another customer — do not embed account IDs in configurations that will outlive the account

In an AWS Organizations context, a member account must be removed from the organization before it can be closed. Member accounts also require their own payment method before closure (they were using consolidated billing from the management account), which must be added to the account directly before the close operation.
Caution:

Never close an account that contains production data you have not confirmed is backed up elsewhere. The 90-day deletion window exists precisely as a safety net, but the safest approach is to migrate or verify all data first, then close the account.
Cross-Account Access Patterns

Once resources live in separate accounts, you need explicit mechanisms to allow access across them. AWS provides two primary patterns: cross-account IAM role assumption and resource-based policies. Understanding both is essential for operating in a multi-account environment.
Cross-Account IAM Role Assumption

The most common cross-account access pattern is IAM role assumption. A role in Account B (the target) has a trust policy that permits Account A (the source) to assume it. An IAM principal in Account A calls
sts:AssumeRole
with the ARN of the role in Account B, receives temporary credentials, and uses those credentials to make API calls as if it were in Account B.
This trust policy on a role in Account B allows the role
DeploymentRole
in Account A (account ID 111111111111) to assume it. The
ExternalId
condition prevents the confused deputy problem — an attacker cannot trick an intermediate service into assuming the role on their behalf unless they also know the external ID.
The caller in Account A needs an IAM policy that grants
sts:AssumeRole
on the specific role ARN in Account B:

This two-sided model — trust policy on the target side plus permission policy on the source side — ensures both accounts must explicitly opt into the cross-account relationship. Neither account alone can establish the trust unilaterally.

import boto3

def assume_cross_account_role(target_account_id: str, role_name: str, session_name: str):
    """Assume a role in another account and return a boto3 session."""
    sts = boto3.client("sts")
    role_arn = f"arn:aws:iam::{target_account_id}:role/{role_name}"

    response = sts.assume_role(
        RoleArn=role_arn,
        RoleSessionName=session_name,
        DurationSeconds=3600,  # 1 hour max for cross-account sessions
    )

    creds = response["Credentials"]
    return boto3.Session(
        aws_access_key_id=creds["AccessKeyId"],
        aws_secret_access_key=creds["SecretAccessKey"],
        aws_session_token=creds["SessionToken"],
    )

# Use the cross-account session
prod_session = assume_cross_account_role(
    target_account_id="222222222222",
    role_name="ReadOnlyAuditRole",
    session_name="SecurityAuditPipeline",
)
s3 = prod_session.client("s3")
buckets = s3.list_buckets()["Buckets"]
print(f"Buckets in prod account: {[b['Name'] for b in buckets]}")
Resource-Based Policies for Direct Cross-Account Access

Some AWS resources (S3 buckets, SQS queues, SNS topics, Lambda functions, KMS keys, Secrets Manager secrets) support resource-based policies that can directly grant access to principals in other accounts without requiring role assumption. This simplifies certain patterns at the cost of distributed policy management.
For example, an S3 bucket in Account B can have a bucket policy that allows Account A's Lambda execution role to read objects directly:
With this bucket policy, the Lambda function in Account A can call
s3.get_object()
on the bucket in Account B directly — no
sts:AssumeRole

needed. The tradeoff: the access grant is defined in Account B's bucket policy, making it harder to audit from Account A's perspective. Role assumption keeps the audit trail unified in CloudTrail under the source account.
Account Vending and Landing Zones

As organizations scale past a handful of accounts, manually creating and configuring each account becomes impractical. Account vending refers to the automated, self-service process of provisioning new AWS accounts with all required baseline configuration already in place.
Landing Zone Concept

A landing zone is a pre-configured, secure multi-account AWS environment. It provides the foundational accounts (management, log archive, security), the OU structure, baseline SCPs, organization-level logging (CloudTrail, Config), and cross-account access roles. Any new account vended into the landing zone inherits all of these configurations automatically, ensuring consistent security posture without manual setup per account.

The landing zone concept was popularized by AWS and codified in AWS Control Tower, but many organizations implement custom landing zones using Infrastructure as Code tools like Terraform or CDK. A custom landing zone gives more control over the OU structure, SCP customization, and integration with enterprise tooling (Active Directory, SIEM systems, ticketing platforms).
Account Factory

AWS Control Tower's Account Factory is an automated workflow that provisions new accounts on request. It integrates with AWS Service Catalog — an account requestor fills out a form (account name, email, OU placement, baseline configuration) and Account Factory creates the account, applies the landing zone guardrails, and registers it in Control Tower's dashboard.
For organizations using Terraform, the
terraform-aws-modules/account-factory

module (and the broader Control Tower Account Factory for Terraform, known as AFT) provides equivalent capability managed as code. AFT lets teams define account customizations in Git repositories, creating an auditable, version-controlled history of what configuration was applied to each account and when.
Account Tagging and Resource Organization

Beyond the account hierarchy itself, AWS accounts can be tagged at the Organizations level. Account tags propagate into billing reports through Cost Allocation Tags, enabling cost attribution by business unit, environment, or application even in a consolidated billing model.

# Tag an account at the Organizations level
aws organizations tag-resource \
  --resource-id 123456789012 \
  --tags Key=Environment,Value=Production Key=Team,Value=Platform Key=CostCenter,Value=Eng-001

# List tags on an account
aws organizations list-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-id 123456789012
Account-level tags are distinct from resource-level tags (tags on EC2 instances, S3 buckets, etc.) but appear alongside them in Cost and Usage Reports. A report showing cost by the
CostCenter
tag across all accounts in the organization lets finance allocate cloud spend to the correct budget center regardless of which account the resource lives in.
Common Account Architecture Patterns
Beyond the basic single-account vs multi-account decision, several well-established account architecture patterns have emerged in the AWS community:
Pattern
Account Structure
Best For
Minimal multi-account
3 accounts: Management, Non-prod, Prod
Small teams needing blast radius isolation between prod and non-prod
Environment per account
Management + Dev + Staging + Prod accounts
Teams where each environment needs independent IAM and quotas
Team-per-account
One account per application team, plus shared services
Enterprises where teams have independent deployment pipelines
AWS Landing Zone (AWS-recommended)
Management + Security tooling + Log archive + Shared services + Workload OUs
Any organization that needs compliance controls and centralized governance
Full Control Tower
Control Tower + Account Factory + customized guardrails
Enterprises that need automated account vending at scale

There is no universal right answer — the appropriate pattern depends on team size, regulatory requirements, operational maturity, and the rate at which new accounts need to be created. A two-person startup can operate effectively with two accounts (personal sandbox + production); a financial services company with 200 engineers may need 100+ accounts organized into a sophisticated OU hierarchy.
Pragmatic guidance:

Start with the simplest multi-account structure that solves your current problem. Separating development and production into two accounts costs almost nothing in operational overhead but delivers meaningful blast radius isolation. Grow the account hierarchy as the team and compliance requirements grow — it is always easier to add accounts than to split a large single-account environment later.
Delegated Administration in AWS Organizations

A challenge in large Organizations is that the management account cannot be used for day-to-day operations — it should remain governance-only with minimal resources. Yet some organization-level services (GuardDuty, Security Hub, Macie, Inspector) traditionally required the management account for centralized administration. AWS addresses this through delegated administration: nominating a member account to act as the administrator for a specific service organization-wide.

The security account (often called the audit account) is typically the delegated administrator for all security services. This concentrates security monitoring responsibilities in a dedicated, tightly controlled account without requiring anyone to log into the management account for daily security operations.

# Delegate GuardDuty administration to the security account
aws guardduty enable-organization-admin-account \
  --admin-account-id 999999999999  # the security account's ID

# Delegate Security Hub administration to the security account
aws securityhub enable-organization-admin-account \
  --admin-account-id 999999999999

# Once delegated, the security account can manage GuardDuty/Security Hub
# across all member accounts without accessing the management account
aws guardduty list-members --detector-id <detector-id>  # run from security account

Delegation is service-specific — you can delegate GuardDuty administration to the security account and S3 Storage Lens administration to a different analytics account. Each service has at most one delegated administrator across the organization.
Account Structure and IAM Identity Center

In a multi-account environment, managing separate IAM users in each account is operationally prohibitive at scale. AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) solves this by providing a single sign-on portal where users authenticate once and access any permitted account through temporary role assumptions.

IAM Identity Center is configured in the management account (or its delegated administrator account). Administrators define Permission Sets — collections of IAM policies that represent a role (ReadOnly, Developer, Administrator) — and then assign users or groups from a directory (AWS-managed, Active Directory, or an external SAML provider like Okta or Azure AD) to specific accounts with specific Permission Sets.

The user experience: a developer opens the IAM Identity Center portal, sees a list of accounts they have access to and the roles available in each, clicks to access the AWS Console in the desired account, or copies short-lived CLI credentials for that account. No per-account passwords, no long-lived access keys, no separate IAM user management per account.

This architecture means the answer to 'how do users access resources in 50 accounts?' is not 'create 50 × N IAM users' — it is 'configure N users in Identity Center with appropriate account assignments, and let temporary credential issuance handle the rest.' The per-account IAM namespace is reserved for service accounts (Lambda execution roles, EC2 instance profiles, CI/CD pipeline roles) rather than human users.
Design principle:

Human access to accounts should flow through IAM Identity Center with time-limited sessions. Machine access (services, automation) should use IAM roles with instance profiles or IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts in EKS). Long-lived IAM user credentials with access keys should not exist in well-architected multi-account environments.
Summary

An AWS account is the fundamental isolation and billing boundary in AWS — a complete container for resources, identities, and configuration with a unique 12-digit Account ID. Single-account architectures are simple and appropriate for small teams, but multi-account architectures using AWS Organizations provide true blast radius isolation, independent service quotas per environment, and centralized governance through Service Control Policies.

Key structural facts: the account boundary is enforced by AWS itself (not just by policies), regions are independently configured within each account, service quotas are per-account per-region, and billing is per-account with optional consolidation through Organizations. Every production workload benefits from at minimum separating development and production into different accounts, with AWS Control Tower providing an accelerated path to setting up the foundational multi-account structure correctly from the start.
